text:
Hey! I'm Ben. I grew up in the 80s in Seattle, logging onto bulletin boards with an inherited Cp/m machine, and started playing guitar and making music with machines at the same time. I started coding for work in the mid 90s after realizing I couldn't do social work in our shelter system here fulltime. What brings me here are a few areas of interest looking for community:

- I've been tinkering with my music composition library Thuja for years. It's an expression of my mental model when I write music. Docs, explainer video and maybe some sort of visual programming or ux layer are on my to do list, but every time I go into composer mode with it, I end up adding or tweaking features. The repo is here <https://github.com/benmca/thuja> and has a workable readme and examples.
I have been a fan of 100rabbits since i first heard about Orca and an enthusiast about and their site has me rethinking this mental model all the time.  Ditto for Tidal Cycles.

- I'm a big journaler and my website is where I keep some thoughts - mostly in the form of a daily sound, some text and a random shot from my phone on that day. After doing this for a few years - and 'purging' a lot of old keeper sketches from my archive onto this sound blog, I've got a feedback loop with myself that feels like a *personal time machine and a self-censorship killer*. Sometimes a post is a field recording, sometimes its a sketch, sometimes its a performance etc.
Right now I'm thinking about how to do describe and apply this do->post->get distance->re-evaluate->re-do cycle to be a frictionless part of my programming flow and anywhere else I learn by doing.
<https://www.listenfaster.com/main/1min/>

- A third topic is that I know I am not alone feeling deeply that programming and being a musician are intertwined in that they come from the same fundamental artistic impulse or thrust and it doesn't make me less of one to be the other. My artistic itch is scratched doing something technical without a sonic or artistic context.  I juggle data pipelines between energy producers and sustainabiliy policy for my day job and sometime feel like a band leader and a composer, at least I'm using the same parts of my brain and skills.  So I'm psyched to connect with other people who are sometimes seen as having feet in different camps.  Hungry for more community :slightly_smiling_face:

Also I thought the Esolang episode of the podcast was rad, and has me moving from lurking to introducer of self today :slightly_smiling_face:

I'll read more on the slack here and get an idea of where to put a discussion, but any pointers to previous relevant threads are appreciated.

text: Lovely stuff on your soundcloud - just hit play.

text: What a fascinating backstory! Thank you for sharing this, and I'm glad you're here. If you haven't seen it, we have a bit of (long-winded, sorry) <https://feelingof.com/member-handbook/#channels|guidance> about what the different channels are for on our website.

elements.text: What a fascinating backstory! Thank you for sharing this, and I'm glad you're here. If you haven't seen it, we have a bit of (long-winded, sorry)
about what the different channels are for on our website.

text:
Hej!

I've always been fascinated by computers ever since I saw the first C64 in my brothers room at the age of 6. Started programming, as most did then, straight away. The first 3 years was basically just 10 print "Mattias"; 20 goto 10. But eventuelly learned for loops, if statements, and transitioned to Amiga and AMOS. Since I wanted to code demos as I found the demoscene the place to be, I quickly picked up assembler and watched bits switch in the debugger for a few years, creating blitter and copper magic, constantly being amazed by how arithmatic and pushing data in memory could cause such magical things happen on screen. Only much later (maybe age 13-14?) did I move to higher order languages such as Pascal and C, and when getting a PC (reluctantly) in 97, I slowly transitioned to C and C++.

That was the start of a life long computing love affair. I kept coding computer graphics mostly until going to university studying computer science, learning the academic vocabulary around computing. At this point, computing was not the tricky part, but what you could do with it that was tricky and fascinating. I think that's what led me to doing a PhD in human-computer interaction, even though I failed the first HCI-class while at uni since it wasn't technical, and apparently people don't respond to bit pushing like a machine does.

I am now an associate professor in interaction design at the University of Gothenburg, Sweden. My academic life has consisted of designing, building, and studying everyday technology such as mobile social media, text messaging, wellbeing apps, wearable technologies, health apps. I'm looking at the effects of technology on everyday practices, trying to understand how technology shape the world, and how we can shape the world through technology. I'm teaching programming, design, and AI primarily.

I'm currently investigating two related things: one is how the practice of programming, and what it means to instruct machines, is changing as a result of LLMs. The other is how LLMs may change the way we interact with computers, seeing an ontological shift of computing on the horizon.

In my spare time i play with my kids, grow tomatoes, and enjoy the occasional read and listen to Bret Victor and Alan Kay. Been listening to FoC podcast for a year or so and enjoy it immensely. Just joined slack, and this reads as a lovely community.

Thanks!
